original_hypothesis.title: ECDSA Signature Malleability Allows Third-Party Nonce Consumption via Alternate Valid Signature
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The _recoverSigner function does not enforce the s-value to be in the lower half of the secp256k1 curve order. For any valid ECDSA signature (r, s, v), a second valid signature (r, N-s, v') exists that recovers to the same address. An attacker who observes a valid voucher signature can compute the malleable variant and submit it, consuming the nonce. While funds flow to the correct payee, the original submitter's transaction would fail.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: OpenZeppelin's ECDSA.recover enforces s-value bounds, but this contract uses a custom _recoverSigner without s-value validation. On a no-mempool chain this is harder to exploit but the code defect exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Custom ecrecover wrapper without s-value check; no require(uint256(s) <= 0x7FFFFFFF...20A0)

preconditions: Attacker obtains a valid signature (e.g., from on-chain settlement events or off-chain leak)
Attacker can submit transaction before original submitter (harder on no-mempool chain)
Nonce has not yet been consumed

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Malleable signatures can be constructed for any valid signature
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing s-value lower-half enforcement per EIP-2

analysis_questions: On a no-mempool chain, how would an attacker observe valid signatures?
Is the nonce consumption the only impact, or can it lead to fund theft?
Does the nonce increment prevent any replay regardless of malleability?
validation_requirements: Construct malleable signature from a valid one
Submit both and verify only one succeeds
Confirm funds go to correct payee regardless of which signature is used

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Payee submits settle() with valid voucher signature. 2. Attacker observes the signature (from event logs after settlement or off-chain). 3. For a future voucher, if attacker can obtain signature before on-chain submission, they compute (r, N-s, 27↔28). 4. Attacker submits settle() with malleable signature, consuming the nonce. 5. Payee's original transaction reverts with NonceTooLow.
prerequisites: Access to valid signature before nonce consumption

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: No direct fund theft — nonce consumed, funds go to correct payee
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Payee still receives payment; only gas is wasted

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for malleable signature acceptance

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Signature s-value analysis on settlement transactions

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
